coredata.dc:title: Great enhancement of red emitting carbon dots with B/Al/Ga doping for dual mode anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Kang
Zhang, Xinghua
Li, Xiang
Qin, Ruohan
Cheng, Yahui
Li, Lanlan
Yang, Xiaojing
Yu, Xiaofei
Lu, Zunming
Liu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Red emitting carbon dots (RCDs) still suffer from low photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and unclear luminescence mechanism. Group IIIA (B, Al, Ga) elements are firstly introduced to improve the QY of RCDs and the QY has been significantly increased from 0.29% to 12.02% (forty one times), 1.99% (seven times) and 1.53% (five times) for B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, respectively. The improvement of QY for RCDs is mainly originated from the formation of CNIIIAO bonds, which suppress the non-radiative transition with electron-withdrawing effects. In addition, B-CDs with acid-induced discoloration can be applied to a new anti-counterfeiting technology with dual mode, and this reversible process of acid-induced discoloration is originated from the reversibly switchable structure between BOH and BCl bonds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Element doping
Quantum yield
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and size distribution of prepared CDs
Structure and chemical bonding states of prepared CDs
Chemical bonding states of acid-induced discoloration for B-CDs
Enhancement and acid-induced discoloration mechanism of CDs
Application of dual mode anti-counterfeiting for B-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme of CDs with different doping elements via solvothermal method.
TEM images and size distribution of (a) RCDs, (b) B-CDs, (c) Al-CDs and (d) Ga-CDs.

(a) C1s spectra and fitting results of RCDs, B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, (b) N1s spectra and fitting curves of RCDs, B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, (c) O1s spectra and fitting results of RCDs, B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, (d) B1s, Al2p, and Ga3d spectra with the related fitting curves, respectively.
(a) Emission spectra, (b) excitation spectra, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (d) decay curves of B-CDs with different amounts of B doping (wt.%).

Emission spectra of B-CDs with adding different (a) HCl contents and (b) EDA amounts at 20 μM HCl addition. UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CDs with adding various (c) HCl contents and (d) EDA amounts at 20 μM HCl addition, respectively.
(a) C1s, (b) N1s, (c) O1s and (d) B1s spectra and fitting results of B-CDs, (e) C1s, (f) N1s, (g) O1s and (h) B1s/Cl2p spectra and fitting curves of B-CDs with HCl treatment.
(a) Schematic structure of RCDs and B-CDs, (b) enhanced and acid-induced discoloration mechanism diagram of group IIIA elements doping for RCDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of PVA film made from B-CDs and B-CDs with HCl under 365 nm excitation. Insets are the photos of B-CDs based PVA film under sunlight and 365 nm excitation. (c) Photos of B-CDsl used for anti-counterfeiting technology with acid treatment.

head.ce:title: Great enhancement of red emitting carbon dots with B/Al/Ga doping for dual mode anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red emitting carbon dots (RCDs) still suffer from low photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and unclear luminescence mechanism. Group IIIA (B, Al, Ga) elements are firstly introduced to improve the QY of RCDs and the QY has been significantly increased from 0.29% to 12.02% (forty one times), 1.99% (seven times) and 1.53% (five times) for B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, respectively. The improvement of QY for RCDs is mainly originated from the formation of CNIIIAO bonds, which suppress the non-radiative transition with electron-withdrawing effects. In addition, B-CDs with acid-induced discoloration can be applied to a new anti-counterfeiting technology with dual mode, and this reversible process of acid-induced discoloration is originated from the reversibly switchable structure between BOH and BCl bonds.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been applied in various fields including white LED, display, fluorescent ink and anti-counterfeiting [1–6] due to its unique properties [7,8] since its discovery in 2004 [9]. Among them, red carbon dots have advantages of deep penetration and low radiation damage [10], and red emission is necessary primary color to achieve white light emitting diodes. However, red CDs still suffer from the low photoluminescence quantum yield (QY). Up to now, some efforts were concentrated on obtaining red CDs with higher QY (3% [11], 8% [12], 24% [13], 16.8% [14], 12.9% [15]), while there is lack of clear mechanism for the improvement of QY for the red CDs. In general, the key to improve QY is to suppress non-radiative transitions, increase the probability of radiation transition, and control the electronic transition process of surface states or defect states. Wang et al. [16] synthesized high crystallinity red CDs with QY of 53% by sequencial dehydrative condensation and dehydrogenative planarization. Xie et al. [17] reported a new kind of red CDs with QY up to 33.7%, and the QY improvement is attributed to less nonradiative transition of rigid conjugated nitrogen groups in graphitic layered structure. Jia et al. [18] obtained electron-donating group passivated CDs with QY up to 86%. The charge transfer excited state in a π-conjugated system resulted from -NR2 passivation plays a key role in affording high QY. Unfortunately, modulation of π-conjugated structure or rigid conjugated nitrogen groups is difficult to realize controllable enhancement of QY for red CDs.

Similar to modulation of π-conjugated structure or rigid conjugated nitrogen groups, n-type heteroatoms in CDs, such as N [19,20], P [21], S [22], F [23], are expected to promote radiation transition, further analysis discloses that these elements usually lead to red shift or longer lifetime of luminescence. Most of them have little effect on the fluorescence intensity except for N elements, and the QY improvement is mainly originated from passivation or specific structure such as pyrrolic N. Therefore, considering the electron-withdrawing effect of group IIIA elements [24], inhibiting non-radiative transitions with p-type doping is most likely to be another way to improve the QY. To the best of our knowledge, there is no systematic investigation of group IIIA elements doping for improving the QY of red CDs. Acquired by accident, B-CDs with dual mode and reversibly switchable emission properties after doping was achieved, which is beneficial for a much higher level of security in anti-counterfeiting since almost all CDs generally have single-mode emission [25–27].

In this work, we synthesized a new kind of red emitting CDs (RCDs) with QY of 0.29% by solvothermal method. Group IIIA elements (B/Al/Ga) were introduced to improve the QY of RCDs and the QY of RCDs increases from 0.29% to 12.02% for B-doped CDs (B-CDs). Al and Ga elements (Al-CDs and Ga-CDs) also show similar enhancement effect as B-CDs. The formation of C–N–IIIA–O bonds accompany with high crystallinity, configurations of the C and N bonding with electron-withdrawing effect can inhibit non-radiative transitions and improve the QY of RCDs. This work provides a universal solution to improve QY of RCDs. In addtion, RCDs with reversibly switchable emission are achieved after B doping, which can be applied to dual mode anti-counterfeiting technology with higher security.

N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and aluminum isopropoxide were obtained from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Company Limited (Tianjin, China). Boric acid and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). O-phenylenediamine was bought from Tianjin Jinke Fine Chemical Research Institute. 1,8-diaminonaphthalene was bought from Macklin Company and gallium isopropoxide was ordered from Alfa Aesar (China). All chemical materials were analytical pure without further purification.

Red carbon dots were synthesized by the solvothermal method. In detail, 0.25 mmol o-phenylenediamine and 0.25 mmol 1,8-diaminonaphthalene were added to 10 mL DMF. Next, the reaction solution was transferred to a 15 mL teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, then diluting the prepared solution four times with DMF. B-doping CDs (B-CDs), Al-doping CDs (Al-CDs) and Ga-doping CDs (Ga-CDs) were obtained in the same way except for adding boric acid, aluminum isopropoxide or gallium isopropoxide during preparation and the amount of doping (wt%) is based on the mass of o-phenylenediamine and naphthalene diamine. The optimal doping amount of boric acid, aluminum isopropoxide and gallium isopropoxide is 150 wt%, 80 wt%, 80 wt%, respectively. To ensure the consistency concentrations of CDs and considering the limited effect of purification on fluorescence characteristics, the purification step was omitted from our synthesis. B-CDs (150 wt%) dispersed in DMF were dried and then dispersed in an equal volume of isopropanol to obtain isopropanol solution of B-CDs.

To fabricate the PVA film based on B-CDs, specific steps are as follows. First, 8 mL isopropanol solution of B-CDs (4.3 g/L) and 0.2 g PVA were dispersed in 4 mL deionized water with bath at 80 °C. Then, the above uniform mixture was added to a self-made mold, and cured at room temperature for 12 h. In the same way, the uniform mixture painted on banknote can be used for anti-counterfeiting.

Morphology of the prepared CDs was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, D8) was used to characterize the phase of the CDs with 2θ scan between 10° and 80°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a spectrometer (Bruker, WQF-410). Raman spectroscopy was measured on a confocal microscopic Raman spectrometer (Lab Ram HR800). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of prepared CDs were taken on a PHI1600EXCA photoelectron spectrometer. The ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were carried out on a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U-3900H). The excitation and emission spectra of CDs were collected by a steady and transient state spectrophotometer (Horiba, FL-3-22).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and size distribution of prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , RCDs and group IIIA elements doped RCDs were prepared via solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene with (without) addition of boric acid, aluminum isopropoxide or gallium isopropoxide as boron, aluminum and gallium sources, respectively. The TEM images of prepared CDs showed in Fig. 2 confirm that the carbon dots were successfully prepared.

B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs own average particle size of 1.6–3.0 nm while RCDs have larger size of 4 nm. It can be seen lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm in the B-CDs (Fig. S1), which is in accordance with the (1 0 0) facets of graphite [11], indicating graphite-like structure contained in this material. According to the size distribution of prepared CDs, the enhancement of luminescence properties for doped RCDs should not be originated from quantum size effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and chemical bonding states of prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effects of structure and chemical bonding states on luminescence performance for prepared CDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD), FTIR, Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed. XRD patterns (Fig. S2) show that there are three obvious diffraction peaks of RCDs around 14.8°, 29.4° and 42.1°, corresponding to amorphous carbon, the crystal faces of carbon at (1 1 0) and (0 2 0), respectively. Compared with the RCDs, the formation of new phase (BN)0.33C0.87 in B-CDs and AlN5O14 in Al-CDs together with their diffraction peak intensity changes, which indicates that B and Al may successfully enter the lattice of the RCDs. The presence of Al2O3 in Al-CDs and Ga2O3 in Ga-CDs is induced by their large atomic radius, which results in less incorporation.

FTIR spectra further prove that doping changes the proportion and types of chemical bonding for RCDs. From Fig. S3a, it can be observed that various bonds are formed in RCDs, including CH (3049 cm−1), NH (3381 cm−1), CN (1640 cm−1), CC (1583 cm−1), CN (1481 cm−1), and CO bonds (1203 cm−1), [28,29] which suggests that RCDs consist of aromatic structure and possess abundant N/O related species on the surface. There are some new bonds appear after doping B, Al or Ga elements. As shown in Fig. S3b, B-CDs display new peaks at 1525, 1417 and 1360 cm−1 which corresponds to CC, NB and CNbonds [30], respectively. The formation of NB bonds further confirms that B elements have been doped into RCDs and greatly change the bonding states of RCDs. It is worth noting that the presence of CN and CNbonds in B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs reveals these CDs contain more contents of graphitic nitrogen (Fig. S3b–d), which is helpful for QY enhancement since these sp2 clusters (graphitic structure) can confine π-electrons, thus leading to more efficient radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs in sp2 clusters [31]. Raman spectra further comfirm the graphitization degree of prepared CDs (Fig. S4), and the intensity ratio of disordered D band at 1309 cm −
1 to the crystalline G band at 1614 cm −
1 (ID/IG) is about 1.40 (RCDs), 1.09 (B-CDs), 1.22 (Al-CDs), 1.32 (Ga-CDs), respectively [32]. These results indicate that group IIIA elements doping can enhance the crystallinity degree, which is helpful to increase the QY of RCDs.

In addition to the increase of crystallinity, it seems that new bonds generation after doping greatly affect the quantum efficiency of RCDs because electron transition process has been changed according to fluorescence lifetime measurements. In accord with Fig. S5, all samples contain C, N, O and relative doping elements (B, Al, Ga). The doping contents are decreased from B to Ga, which is attributed to the increase of atomic radius (Table S1). Fine scan XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O 1s and doping elements (B1s, Al2p, Ga 3d) for the CDs are given in Fig. 3 . The C1s spectra of prepared CDs can be well fitted with three Gauss curves centered at 284.5, 285.7, and 288.6 eV, which are respectively corresponded to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO bonds [33], respectively. The relative contents of CO bonds slightly decrease after doping and lead to the blue shift of emission, which reveals that the red emission of CDs is related to CO bonds. The N1s spectra can be fitted well with four Gauss curves centered at 398.4, 399.2, 400.2 and 401.6 eV, corresponding to pyridinic, amino, pyrrolic and graphitic N [28–30], respectively. Compared with RCDs, the peak shifts to the high binding energy in the N1s spectra of B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, which indicates that there is a strong electron-withdrawing group or an electron-withdrawing compound around the N atoms [34,35]. Therefore, this is a sign that boron has been doped into CDs successfully as p-type dopant. The increase of graphitic N in B-CDs suggests that the N species play an important role in enhancing emission. Although the presence of CN and CNbonds in B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs reveals these CDs contain more contents of graphitic nitrogen. However, the relative peak intensity of CN and CNbonds is much different compared with the CC bonds, which indicates that the content of graphite nitrogen in these three kinds of CDs is very different, thus leading to the absence of graphite nitrogen in N1s spectra. O1s spectra contain two common components associated with CO at 531.8 eV and CO at 533.8 eV [33], and other bonds associated with BO at 532.6 eV, AlO at 530.5 eV, and GaO at 530.8 eV for B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, respectively. For B1s, Al2p and Ga3d spectra, they can be fitted well with B–N/BO (190.1 eV, 192 eV) [36,37], AlN/AlO (73.1 eV/75.2 eV) [38,39], and GaN/GaO (17.0 eV/20.2 eV) [40,41] bonds, respectively. The detailed chemical bonds content (%) in RCDs, B-CDs, Al-CDs, Ga-CDs is shown in Table S2. Taking XPS results together, and the total energy of CNBOH is much lower than that of NCBOH when B-OH is near the edge site of N [42], it is safe to rule out that the doped boron elements exist in the form of CNBO bonds. Aluminum has high affinity with oxygen functional groups to form stable chelator complex [43] and given raw material (N,N-dimethylformamide and aluminum isopropoxide) is a rich source for oxygenated groups, it provides anchoring sites for aluminum to incorporate into the carbon structure through oxygen groups and thereby increase the doping efficiency [44,45]. It has been proved that the N/Al co-doped CDs contain AlO, AlN and AlOH after doping [38], which is consistent with our results. In addition, another mental dopant such as magnesium, is has also been used to improve the photoluminescence of CDs and MgN and MgO are also confirmed to exist in Mg-CDs [46], which suggests that it is possible for large size of metals to be doped into the CDs, and dopants (M) usually form the chemical bonds of MO and MN. Based on our experimental results that there are AlN, AlO and GaN, GaO in the Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, it can be seen the way of forming chemical bonds is similar to B-N and B-O in B-CDs since their same number of valency electrons. Therefore, we can conclude that Al and Ga are existed in the form of CNAlOH and CN–GaOH bonds at edge sites in Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, respectively. This chemical structure is the main cause for the promotion of luminescence performance.

Optical properties of RCDs are shown in Fig. S6, the RCDs have single red emission peak at 595 nm with two excitation peaks at 452 nm and 563 nm. In addition, RCDs exhibit excitation independent feature under different excitation wavelengths and it can be prepared at very low reaction temperature (120 °C, Fig. S7), which indicates that prepared RCDs are ideal red CDs except for its low QY. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of RCDs (Fig. S6aa) shows several absorption peaks centered at 275 nm, 340 nm and 525 nm, which are assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bonds in carbon network, n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN bonds, respectively [33,47]. The absorption peak at 525 nm is probably related to the red emission. The absorption characteristics suggest that the emission of RCDs is closely related to the energy states formed by N/O species. The acid-base treatment results further confirm most energy states are on the surface of RCDs since the red emission intensity decreases obviously under strong acidic/alkaline conditions (Fig. S8). We also used o-phenylenediamine (O-CDs) or 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (D-CDs) as single raw material to prepared O-CDs and D-CDs with the same synthesis condition, respectively. The emission spectra (Fig. S9a) suggest that the emission peak of O-CDs is 520 nm and D-CDs almost have no fluorescence, which is different from the RCDs. The emission spectra of RCDs purified by dialysis (1000 Da) also indicate that there is no obvious photoluminescence difference (blueshift with 2 nm) between RCDs and dialysis RCDs with purification except for the emission intensity (Fig. S9b). The change of emission intensity is mainly due to the concentration change of RCDs caused by dialysis. Therefore, it can be concluded that the optical properties of RCDs originate from RCDs themselves but not from the fluorescent molecular byproducts.

Although the impurity levels introduced by N/O species are beneficial to obtain red emission, it will also generate many non-radiative transition centers, resulting in the low QY of RCDs. Therefore, even if the CDs with red emission have been obtained successfully, it still suffers from the low QY of 0.29%, which greatly limits its applications. Group IIIA elements (B, Al, Ga) were selected to improve the QY of RCDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , the doping of group IIIA elements has greatly improved the QY of RCDs. For instance (Fig. 4a), the emission intensity of B-CDs increases continuously with the increase of B contents and finally achieves about twenty times with 150 wt% (atm.%[Boron] = 11.43% in CDs) B source under 440 nm excitation, the emission spectrum of B-CDs is shown in Fig. S10. At the same time, the red emission peak slightly blue shifts from 595 nm to 587 nm, which should be induced by B-doping effect and will be explained in detail below. Fig. 4b displays the excitation spectra of B-CDs, and it can be seen that the changes of excitation spectra for B-CDs are consistent with the emission spectra. The excitation intensity is also enhanced about twenty times with the increase of B doping and its excitation peak at 452 nm is red shifted to 456 nm while the other excitation peak is blue shifted from 563 nm to 550 nm. The effects of reaction times and temperatures on luminescence intensity are also investigated (Fig. S11), which finds that the optimal reaction temperature and time is 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction time and temperature have less influence on emission intensity, which disclose the change of QY is mainly induced by a certain stable structure with B doping. In addition, Al and Ga doping also effectively increase the emission intensity of RCDs. As shown in Fig. S12, the emission intensity of Al-CDs increases about five times and its two excitation peaks are shifted to middle of them with increased Al doping. The emission intensity of Ga-CDs (Fig. S13) is enhanced about six times and the shift trend of two excitation peaks toward the middle is more obvious. The above results suggest that group IIIA elements doping can effectively increase the luminescence intensity of RCDs, and the QY of RCDs is greatly increased from 0.29% to 12.02%, 1.99% and 1.58% for B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, respectively, which reveals that the improvement of the luminescence intensity for RCDs may be based on the same mechanism. A possible explanation for attenuation of the enhancement effect can be attributed to non-metallic enhancement of doping elements. Contrary to expectations, it does not find a significant difference of UV–Vis absorbance (Figs. 4c, S14) between RCDs and group IIIA elements doped RCDs, and the absorption peak at 525 nm is even decreased after introducing B, Al or Ga elements. The reason is doping only partially changes the bonding ratios of RCDs and the main structure basically remains unchanged. The electron transition process is evidenced by the fluorescence lifetime measurements. The decay curves of prepared CDs can be fitted well with double exponential function and two lifetimes with t1 and t2 were obtained (Table S3). As shown in Fig. 4d, the lifetimes of B-CDs are increased from 0.9 ns to 7.8 ns for t1 and 6 ns to 10.5 ns for t2 with increasing B contents. The lifetimes of Al-CDs (Fig. S5b) and Ga-CDs (Fig. S15c) are also increased after doping, as shown in Table S3. These results suggest that the residence time of the electrons in the excited state is prolonged after doping group IIIA elements, further promoting the enhancement of QY. As for the changes of the electron transition process, we need to further analyze the electronic structure of RCDs to gain an in-depth understanding of the mechanism.

One unanticipated finding is that B-CDs not only have high QY, but also possess acid-induced discoloration in isopropanol. As shown in Fig. 5 a, B-CDs only have single emission peak at 598 nm with no HCl addition while a new emission peak within 633–655 nm appears along with decreased intensity of 598 nm emission after adding 0–20 μL HCl solution (1 mol/L) into isopropanol solution (3 mL) of B-CDs (B-CDs-HCl). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CDs (Fig. 5c) also undergo violently changes and new absorption peaks at 560 and 601 nm appear with increasing contents of HCl. The acid-induced discoloration of B-CDs is probably related to the two new absorption peaks. Besides hydrochloric acid, nitric acid can also achieve the same acid-induced discoloration effect (Fig. S16). Interestingly, this is a completely reversible process for B-CDs which can restore as ever after adding EDA (ethylenediamine), as shown in Fig. 5b and d. Moreover, this property of acid-induced discoloration has good cycle characteristics (Fig. S17). Unlike B-CDs, there is no acid-induced discoloration for RCDs (Fig. S18), indicating this feature is originated from B doping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical bonding states of acid-induced discoloration for B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical structures of B-CDs and B-CDs-HCl are also investigated by XPS and FTIR, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S19, respectively. FTIR spectra display that CN bonds of B-CDs disappear after acid treatment (Fig. S19). XPS spectra (Fig. 6) show more differences between B-CDs and B-CDs-HCl. For Cl2p, it can be fitted well with H–Cl (197.5 eV) and BCl bonds (198.9 eV) [48,49], indicating that OH linked to B atoms possibly reacted with HCl, and HCl and BCl bonds are respectively corresponded to residual acid and reaction products. Compared with

B-CDs, pyrrolic N of B-CDs-HCl disappears and the amounts of B–O bonds also decrease while the contents of BN bonds increase dramatically. The changes of pyrrolic N disclose the amounts of pyridinic N increase, which lead to much more quinone structure for red emission [50]. The changes of BO bonds further reveal that the oxygen-containing functional groups attached to B possibly react with the acid. The relatively increased amounts of BN bonds can be attributed to the decrease of BO bonds. It is these chemical bonding changes that lead to the acid-induced discoloration of B-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement and acid-induced discoloration mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Combining all the factors, schematic structure of prepared CDs with group IIIA element doping and the possible enhancement mechanism are shown in Fig. 7 . The red emission of RCDs is originated from the surface states formed by N/O species in CDs, [51] but N/O species also form some surface defect states which are served as non-radiative transition center, resulting in the low QY. Doping leads to the increase of crystallinity and the formation of CNIIIAO bonds according to chemical structure analysis, so IIIA group elements can improve the QY in two aspects. On the one hand, the group IIIA elements doping can increase the crystallinity through changing the configurations of the C and N bonding status into graphitic structure, which leads to more efficient radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs in sp2 clusters [17,42,52]. On the other hand, the formation of CNIIIAO bonds can suppress the non-radiative transition due to the electron-withdrawing effects introduced by p-type doping [24]. And group IIIA elements tend to change the types and proportions of chemical bonds, leading to less fraction of N/O related defects, thus partially eliminating these non-radiative transition centers. In addition, the new impurity levels induced by doping is the reason of blue shift in emission. The differences of QY enhancement among the three elements are originated from the diffrences of atom radius and metallicity. Larger atom radius means only a few atoms can enter into carbon core of RCDs, and increasing metallicity results in attenuated electron-withdrawing effects. Based on the above analysis, it can be asserted that the combination of high crystallinity and formaion of CNIIIAO bonds contribute to the improvement of QY. This enhancement mechanism is universal for other red CDs, we succeefully enhance the red emission intensity of RCDs-2 reported in literature [33] with B doping (Fig. S20).

Acid-induced discoloration mechanism of B-CDs is also shown in Fig. 7. As HCl is added to the solution of B-CDs, OH groups linked to B atoms react with HCl and thus forming a metastable structure with the Cl− ions, which produce a new energy level corresponding to the emission within 635–665 nm. Here, the main role of Cl− ions is to balance the valence of ions, so this metastable structure can be transformed into OH by hydrolysis reaction after re-addition of the alkali, which leads to a reversible process of acid-induced discoloration. In addition, the increase of pyridine N also causes the red shift of emission [50]. This reversible structure is mainly originated from B-doping since other acids can also cause acid-induced surface state, and Cl− ions only play a role in balancing charge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of dual mode anti-counterfeiting for B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Both B-CDs and acid treated B-CDs have excellent stability, they not only can be presented in organic solvents, but also can be dispersed in PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) solution. As shown in Fig. S21, PVA film made from B-CDs exhibits extremely similar photoluminescence with B-CDs in isopropanol except that red emission blue shift from 598 nm to 585 nm due to different polarity between isopropanol and PVA solution, while PVA film made from acid treated B-CDs appears emission peak within

630–655 nm and the emission intensity is decreased. Fig. 8 a shows emission spectra of PVA film made from B-CDs (film-B-CDs) and HCl treated B-CDs (film-B-CDs-HCl) under 365 nm excitation, the color of film-B-CDs is pink while that of film-B-CDs-HCl is blue. Fortunately, B-CDs in PVA also retain the characteristic of acid-induced discoloration (Fig. 8b), film-B-CDs-HCl also can become pink from blue under 365 nm with alkali treatment since the recovery of red emission at 585 nm, just like B-CDs in isopropanol. According to the acid-induced discoloration, B-CDs can be applied to a new anti-counterfeiting technology with higher security. As shown in Fig. 8b, the PVA solution with B-CDs-HCl was painted on banknote, and it can be seen blue pattern of “HEBUT” under 365 nm excitation, and the blue pattern of “HEBUT” will turn to pink after alkali treatment. Meanwhile, patterns of stars and “C-DOTs” made from B-CDs exhibit pink fluorescence under 365 nm excitation, they can also show blue fluorescence after acid treatment (Fig. 8c). This dual mode anti-counterfeiting technology with B-CDs is promising for practical application with higher security.

In summary, we successfully developed a new solvothermal synthesis of red emitting CDs with QY of 0.29%. Group IIIA (B, Al, Ga) elements are first introduced to improve the QY of RCDs and the QY significantly increases from 0.29% to 12.02%, 1.99% and 1.53% for B-CDs, Al-CDs and Ga-CDs, respectively. The group IIIA elements doping can increase the crystallinity through changing the configurations of the C and N bonding status, which leads to more efficient radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs in sp2 clusters. Furthermore, the formation of CNIIIAO bonds can suppress the non-radiative transition due to the electron-withdrawing effects, thus improving the QY of RCDs. In addition, a reversible process of acid-induced discoloration of B-CDs has been achieved due to the formation of reversibly switchable structure between B and Cl, which can be applied to a new dual mode anti-counterfeiting technology with higher security. This work provides a universal method for improving red emission carbon dots, promoting the wider application of carbon dots.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51771068, 51671079, 51771067, 51871088), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2018202082), and Key Laboratory of Display Materials and Photoelectric Devices, Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.125487.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
